prose:
Fukui reactivity indices for aniline (C6H7N)

Method provenance
Property: atom‐condensed Fukui functions f⁺, f⁻, dual descriptor; most electrophilic/nucleophilic sites.
Level of theory: DFT, tier "standard" → B3LYP/def2-TZVP (no density fitting) in PySCF.
Geometry provenance: user-supplied aniline.xyz; single-point evaluation (no re-optimization) → notation DFT//input-XYZ.
Solvent / environment: gas phase (no implicit solvent).
Charge = 0, multiplicity = 1 (closed-shell singlet parent).  N–1 and N+1 states run as doublets (charge ±1, mult = 2) per skill default.
Software: chemkit fukui-reactivity skill (engine subcommand `fukui`) calling PySCF backend.
Convergence & checks: SCFs for N, N±1 states converged (integrity.status = "ok").  Engine returned three warnings (verbatim below).  All checks in integrity block marked ok; integrity.trustworthy = true.
Live log & files: calculation streamed to a live `.out` log (path printed at job launch; can be tailed in real time).  Result JSON written to /home/erobles/chem-skills/.fidelity_scratch/tmp1htwqpjk/live.json; bar-chart PNG at /home/erobles/chem-skills/benchmarks/fidelity/fukui-reactivity-validation/aniline/argo_o3/20260704-175435_aniline_fukui_reactivity/agent_call_02_plot.png.  Full CLI invocation recorded in the JSON (see `cli_invocation`).

Headline results (Mulliken scheme)
• Most electrophilic site: atom 3 (C) f⁺ = 2.2975867
• Most nucleophilic site: atom 1 (N) f⁻ = 0.20452359

Verbatim warnings returned by chemkit
1. sum(f+) = 1.0000 (should be ~1.0; large drift suggests an N±1 state did not converge)
2. sum(f-) = 1.0000 (should be ~1.0; large drift suggests an N±1 state did not converge)
3. common header present

Interpretation
The carbon at position 3 is predicted to be the primary electrophilic center (largest f⁺), while the ring-bonded nitro­gen (atom 1) is the most nucleophilic (largest f⁻) under gas-phase conditions at the B3LYP/def2-TZVP level.  The summed Fukui functions match the expected charge conservation (Σf⁺ ≈ Σf⁻ ≈ 1), indicating self-consistency of the N, N±1 charge states.
